body: After computing `v` on a miss, the code does `TryAdd` and then unconditionally reads `cache.[idx]`, which forces another lookup. Consider returning `v` when `TryAdd` succeeds (and only falling back to reading from the dictionary when it fails due to a concurrent add). This keeps the concurrent behavior but avoids extra work on every miss.

`cache.TryGetValue idx` uses the F# out-arg-to-tuple translation, which allocates a tuple on every cache access (including hits). Since this cache is likely hot, consider using the byref overload (`let mutable res = Unchecked.defaultof<_>; if cache.TryGetValue(idx, &res) then res else ...`) to avoid per-call allocations. Also, after `TryAdd` you can return `v` directly when the add succeeds to avoid a second dictionary lookup.

`GetInterfaces()` now returns the cached `Type[]` instance. Previously it allocated a fresh array on each call, so callers could (intentionally or accidentally) mutate the returned array without affecting subsequent calls. To avoid sharing a mutable array across callers, consider returning a copy (while still caching the resolved interface `Type`s) or otherwise ensuring the returned collection can't be mutated by consumers.
```suggestion
        override __.GetInterfaces() = interfaces.Value |> Array.copy
```

`mkCacheGeneric` also executes `f idx` inside the lock. This can unnecessarily serialize expensive cache-miss computations and increase contention (especially because these caches are frequently nested inside other cached reads). Prefer locking only around `TryGetValue`/write-back (compute outside the lock after a miss) or use a `ConcurrentDictionary<'T, Lazy<_>>` pattern to keep thread-safety without blocking unrelated lookups.
```suggestion
                // First, try to read from the cache under the lock
                let mutable found = false
                let mutable cachedValue = Unchecked.defaultof<_>
                lock syncObj (fun () ->
                    match cache.TryGetValue idx with
                    | true, cached ->
                        found <- true
                        cachedValue <- cached
                    | false, _ -> ())
                if found then
                    cachedValue
                else
                    // Compute the value outside the lock to avoid serializing expensive work
                    let v = f idx
                    // Write back under the lock, with a second check in case another thread won the race
                    lock syncObj (fun () ->
                        match cache.TryGetValue idx with
                        | true, cached -> cached
                        | false, _ ->
                            cache.[idx] <- v
                            v)
```

`mkCacheInt32` holds the lock while executing `f idx`. Since `f` performs the full row decode (and may call into other caches), this serializes work across threads and can significantly reduce parallelism under parallel compilation. Consider limiting the lock to dictionary access only (double-check pattern: read under lock; compute outside; write back under lock), or switching to a `ConcurrentDictionary<int32, Lazy<_>>`/`Lazy`-based approach so only cache insertion is synchronized while the expensive computation happens without blocking other cache hits.
Why `netstandard2.1` target is needed?
The source of an "Assembly name" is never user-typed. It's always hardcoded to a DLL, and read from an assembly, thus case-sensitivity is irrelevant and an exact match is ok.

The bestGuess logic relies on matching assembly FullName values; consider using a normalized or case-insensitive comparison if variations in assembly naming conventions are possible, to avoid unexpected lookup failures.
```suggestion
                let bestGuess = 
                    asms |> Seq.tryFind(fun a -> String.Equals(a.FullName, t.Assembly.FullName, StringComparison.OrdinalIgnoreCase))
                    |> Option.bind(fun a -> tryGetTypeFromAssembly toTgt t.Assembly.FullName fullName a)
```

No as I understand things that's not correct.

.NET Standard 2.0 TPDTC still have to multi-target, that is, they may be used in a project whose target reference assemblies are .NET Standard 2.0, .NET Standard 2.1, .NET CoreApp 3.0 or whatever else.   They still have to generate code correctly for the correct set of target reference assemblies.  This includes generative assemblies.  

So while TPDTC are .NET Standard 2.0 components, they are in fact code generators for a whole range of target frameworks depending on the reference assembly set they are given. That's what this code is testing, and what type remapping is for.  

To put it another way - there's nothing about the simplification of the engineering assumptions in this repo that removes the requirement that TPDTC still have to implement type remapping.

Two things on this:

1. It was never correct, and `AfterTargets` was always the correct thing to do
2. Neither this nor `AfterTargets` is actually required with `IsFSharpDesignTimeProvider` specified, which was another goody that @KevinRansom showed me and has been around forever (but unused due to the template specifying things the "hard way" that I removed here)

This creates the exact same layout that was specified before. It is a property that exists in the F# compiler targets (and apparently has been there a very long time). @KevinRansom showed it to me and it greatly simplifies this requirement

e.g. 

```
    <!-- We place the TPDTC DLLs in a well-known locations with respect to the runtime DLL -->
    <Copy SourceFiles="..\BasicProvider.DesignTime\bin\$(Configuration)\netstandard2.0\BasicProvider.DesignTime.dll" DestinationFolder="$(OutputPath)\typeproviders\fsharp41\netstandard2.0" />
    <Copy SourceFiles="..\BasicProvider.DesignTime\bin\$(Configuration)\netstandard2.0\FSharp.TypeProviders.SDK.dll" DestinationFolder="$(OutputPath)\typeproviders\fsharp41\netstandard2.0" />
```
This AfterBuild is necessary to place the TPDTC in the right location with respect to the TPRTC outputs.
See other comment.  Probably best to pin this to 4.7.2
Is there a reason for removing this?

I think this should pin to FSHarp.Core 4.7.2 exactly.

TPDTC must load into, for example, VS2019 devenv.exe and fsc.exe.  These use an FSharp.Core that is 4.7.2.  We do not want to encourage people to randomly move to higher FSharp.Core dependencies for their TPDTC since if they do their component will not be loadable into VS2019, and will randomly become unloadable in F# tooling each time they `paket update`.

Like all design time plugins, TPDTC should in general use the *lowest* FSHarp.Core and *lowest* framework dependency they can, in order to load into as many contexts as possible.

This output path is crucial (or the output DLL and PDB must somehow be copied to this location).  Without it the type provider package will not have its design time tooling in the right place.  Perhaps this has been moved somewhere else?

There is a huge amount of important testing being deleted here.  

Type providers still have to correctly cross-target (they can be used in netstandard2.0, netstandard2.1, netcoreapp3.0, net5 projects etc. etc) - this doesn't change just because we assume TPRTC are netstandard 2.0/2.1.  So all this testing of corner cases in cross-targeting is still relevant.

In general, for performance reasons, each of these uses of `SpecificCall` should be lifted to a helper pattern, defined at the module level, e.g.

```
let (|GreaterThan|_|) = (|SpecificCall|_|) <@ (>) @>
```
This prevents the repeated creation of a quotation for `<@ (>) @>` and, IIRC, the implementation of SpecificCall does some pre-processing on the partial application.
